methods: 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Cell culture Mouse P19C5 stem cells, derived from the P19 embryonal carcinoma stem cell line ( Lau and Marikawa 2014 ), were cultured in a medium consisting of 90% Minimum Essential Medium Alpha (no nucleosides), 7.5% newborn calf serum, 2.5% fetal bovine serum, 50 U/mL penicillin, and 50 μg/mL streptomycin (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA). Three-dimensional (3D) elongating cell aggregates, referred to as gastruloids, were generated using the hanging drop method as described previously ( Lau and Marikawa 2014 ). Briefly, cells were dissociated with TrypLE Express (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and suspended at a density of 10 cells/μL in culture medium containing 1% dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) with a specified concentration of the test chemical. The inclusion of 1% DMSO, which is necessary for inducing gastruloid formation, does not exert toxicity on P19C5 cells ( Lau and Marikawa 2014 ). Drops (20 μL each) of the cell suspension were placed onto the inner surface of petri dish lids (16 drops per dish) and inverted to create hanging drops. Human H9 embryonic stem (ES) cells (WA09; NIH registration number 0062) were obtained from WiCell Research Institute (Madison, WI) and cultured under feeder-free conditions in mTeSR1 medium (Stemcell Technologies, Vancouver, BC, Canada) supplemented with 40 U/mL penicillin and 40 μg/mL streptomycin. Culture flasks were pre-coated with iMatrix-511 (Takara Bio, Mountain View, CA) before use. 3D aggregates of H9 cells were generated in round-bottom 96-well plates as described previously ( Marikawa et al. 2020 ). Briefly, cells were dissociated with TrypLE Express and suspended at a density of 20 cells/μL in a medium comprising 80% Minimum Essential Medium Alpha (no nucleosides), 20% 5X mTeSR1 Supplement, 10 μM CHIR99021 (Calbiochem, La Jolla, CA), 2 μM SB431542 (Stemcell Technologies), and 2 nM retinoic acid (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO), with or without a test chemical. A 50 μL aliquot of the cell suspension was pipetted into each well to generate cell aggregates ( Marikawa et al. 2020 ). The procedures to generate gastruloids are depicted in Fig 1B . Although both mouse and human gastruloids exhibit similar elongation morphogenesis, their preparation methods differ in culture media, duration, and cell aggregation techniques. These procedures follow previously established and validated protocols for developmental toxicity testing ( Warkus and Marikawa 2017 ; Marikawa et al. 2020 ; Huntsman et al. 2024 ). 2.2. Test Chemicals The following chemicals were used in this study: amiodarone, N-desethylamiodarone, dronedarone, N-desbutyl dronedarone (Cayman Chemical, Ann Arbor, MI), all- trans retinoic acid (RA) and sodium iodide (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO). Stock solutions of each compound, except for sodium iodide, were prepared in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) at a concentration of 10 mM and stored at −20°C. Sodium iodide was dissolved in water at 2 M as a stock solution. Prior to each experiment, the stock solutions were diluted in culture medium to the desired final concentrations. To evaluate the effects of these chemicals on gastruloid development, cells were exposed to the compounds continuously from the initiation of cell aggregation through the entire culture period (4 days for the mouse model, 5 days for the human model). The range of concentrations tested were established through pilot studies and designed to include both a low dose that had no observable impact on morphology or gene expression and a high dose that produced measurable effects. For clinical context, therapeutically relevant plasma concentrations of amiodarone and dronedarone are summarized in Table 1 . The typical steady-state plasma levels following therapeutic dosing are approximately 1.3-2.6 μM for amiodarone and 0.15-0.30 μM for dronedarone ( Brennan et al. 1991 ; Lafuente-Lafuente et al. 2009 ; Patel et al. 2009 ). 2.3. Morphometric analysis The morphology of chemical-treated gastruloids was analyzed as previously described ( Warkus and Marikawa 2017 ). Briefly, at the end of the culture period, cell aggregates were photographed, and their morphological parameters were quantified using the ImageJ software (Schneider et al. 2012). Three parameters were analyzed: area, elongation distortion index (EDI), and aspect ratio. The area was used as a proxy for overall size of cell aggregates, whereas EDI and aspect ratio are shape descriptors used to measure the extent of elongation. EDI is calculated as (perimeter 2 / 4π × area − 1), as described previously ( Marikawa et al. 2009 ). It quantifies the extent to which a shape deviates from a perfect circle: an EDI of zero corresponds to a perfect circle, and higher values indicate increasing elongation or distortion. Aspect ratio is calculated in ImageJ by fitting an ellipse to the object and dividing the length of its major axis by that of its minor axis. It reflects the overall extent of elongation: an AR of 1 corresponds to a perfect circle, and higher values indicate greater elongation. To facilitate comparisons across experiments, the area, EDI, and adjusted aspect ratio (= aspect ratio − 1) values were normalized in each experiment against the average values of control aggregates. These normalized metrics were expressed as relative area, relative EDI, and relative aspect ratio, respectively, and are reported as percentages, with the mean of control aggregates set to 100%. Data from all replicates were compiled and are presented in figures as mean ± 95% confidence interval (CI). Consistent with previously established criteria ( Warkus and Marikawa 2017 ; Huntsman et al. 2024 ), an adverse effect on gastruloid morphology was defined as statistically significant reduction in relative area by more than 20% compared to the control, or a change in relative EDI or aspect ratio exceeding 40%. 2.4. Cell viability assay Effects of chemical treatments on cell proliferation and viability were assessed using the CellTiter-Glo Luminescent Cell Viability Assay System (Promega, Madison, WI), as described previously ( Warkus et al. 2016 ). Briefly, P19C5 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1,000 cells per well in 100 μL of culture medium containing 0.2% DMSO with the test compound. After 2 days of culture, cells were incubated with a 1:1 mixture of CellTiter-Glo Reagent and phosphate-buffered saline. Following 15-min incubation, luminescence was measured using a Gene Light 55 Luminometer (Microtech, Chiba, Japan). The luminescent signal, which reflects ATP levels as a quantitative proxy for metabolically active cells, was normalized to the sum of all dilution series within each experiment. Data are presented as relative light units, with vehicle control (0 μM) set to 100%. All experiments were performed independently four times, and results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation. 2.5. Quantitative reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) qRT-PCR was performed to quantify the relative expression levels of developmental regulator genes. Procedures for RNA extraction, cDNA synthesis, real-time PCR, and data analysis followed established protocols ( Kirkwood-Johnson et al. 2021 ). The functional roles and PCR primer sequences of the analyzed mouse genes are listed in Table 2 . Previous studies have demonstrated that the expression of these genes is dysregulated by chemical exposures that impair mouse gastruloid morphogenesis ( Lau and Marikawa 2014 ; Li and Marikawa 2015 , 2016 , 2020 ; Yuan and Marikawa 2017 ; Kim and Marikawa 2018 ; Warkus and Marikawa 2018 ; Kirkwood-Johnson et al. 2021 ; Huntsman and Marikawa 2025 ). To assess the adverse impact of drug exposure in the human morphogenesis model, the Altered Level of Embryogenesis Regulator Transcript (ALERT) score was calculated as previously defined ( Marikawa et al. 2020 ). The ALERT score represents the number of genes whose transcript levels are significantly altered by more than 2-fold (either increase or decrease) relative to controls in response to a given chemical exposure. A previous study reported that positive ALERT scores show a 92.9% concordance with the in vivo developmental toxicity of chemical exposures, underscoring the reliability of this metric for identifying adverse effects ( Marikawa et al. 2020 ). Primer sequences for the 15 developmental regulator genes used in the ALERT score analysis are provided in the same reference, while sequences for additional human genes examined in this study are listed in the Supplementary Table . 2.6. Microarray analysis Total RNA was extracted from control, amiodarone-treated, and dronedarone-treated Day 5 human ES cell aggregates (20 aggregates per sample) using the Direct-zol RNA Microprep Kit (Zymo Research, Irvine, CA) with DNase treatment. Subsequent processing of RNA samples was carried out at the Genomics and Bioinformatics Shared Resource, University of Hawaii Cancer Center. RNA integrity was evaluated using Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer with RNA Nano Chip (Agilent, Santa Clara, CA), ensuring that only samples with an RNA Integrity Number (RIN) above 7 were used for further analysis. For microarray hybridization, total RNA (50–100 ng per sample) was processed according to the GeneChip WT PLUS Reagent Kit protocol (ThermoFisher Scientific), which includes reverse transcription and amplification steps to generate sense-strand cDNA. The resulting cDNA was hybridized to Clariom S Human Arrays. Arrays were subsequently washed, stained, and scanned using the GeneChip Fluidics Station 450 and GeneChip Scanner (ThermoFisher Scientific). Resulting CEL files were normalized using the SST-RMA-GENE-level algorithm in the Transcriptome Analysis Console (TAC) software (ThermoFisher Scientific). Three biological replicates were analyzed, each consisting of control, amiodarone-treated (4 μM), and dronedarone-treated (0.5 μM) groups derived from the same batch of cell collection. All data were exported from the TAC software to Microsoft Excel for downstream statistical analyses. Gene Ontology (GO) term enrichment analysis was performed using the Gene Ontology Resource ( Ashburner et al. 2000 ; Thomas et al. 2022 ; Aleksander et al. 2023) to identify biological processes, molecular functions, and cellular components enriched in the differentially expressed genes. 2.7. Statistics All experiments were performed at least three times using independently prepared batches of cells as biological replicates. For morphological analysis, mean values of relative area, relative EDI, and relative aspect ratio, were compared across treatment groups, including controls, using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). When significant differences were detected, post hoc two-sample t-tests were conducted to compare specific groups. Statistical significance for morphological endpoints was defined as p < 0.01. For gene expression analysis by qRT-PCR, mean relative expression levels were compared among groups using one-way ANOVA, followed by post hoc Tukey’s HSD tests to compare control and chemical-treated groups. A threshold of p < 0.05 was used to define statistically significant differences in gene expression, unless otherwise specified. For microarray analysis, p-values for each gene probe were calculated using t-test by comparing control and drug-treated samples across three biological replicates. These p-values were adjusted for multiple comparisons using the false discovery rate (FDR) method to obtain q-values ( Storey and Tibshirani 2003 ). Genes with q-values <0.05 and fold changes greater than 1.5 were considered differentially expressed genes (DEGs). For GO enrichment analysis, GO terms with enrichment greater than 10-fold and q-value less than 0.05 were considered significantly enriched and relevant to this study.
results: 3. Results 3.1. Morphological impact of amiodarone and its major metabolite on mouse gastruloids To investigate the embryotoxic effects of amiodarone, we utilized mouse gastruloids — 3D aggregates of P19C5 pluripotent stem cells that undergo axial elongation morphogenesis in vitro. Cell aggregates were treated with amiodarone or its major metabolite, N-desethylamiodarone, at varying concentrations for 4 days, followed by morphological assessment. Gastruloids treated with amiodarone exhibited a progressive reduction in size and elongation with increasing concentrations ( Fig. 2A ). A similar dose-dependent trend was observed for N-desethylamiodarone, which also diminished gastruloid growth and elongation ( Fig. 2B ). These effects were quantified through morphometric analyses using three parameters, specifically area, elongation distortion index (EDI), and aspect ratio, following a previously established protocol ( Huntsman et al. 2024 ; see Materials and methods ). Consistent with visual observations, all three parameters decreased progressively with increasing concentrations of both amiodarone and N-desethylamiodarone ( Fig. 2C ). Based on predefined adverse effect criteria, namely a reduction in relative area greater than 20% or a change in relative EDI or relative aspect ratio exceeding 40% compared to the control ( Huntsman et al. 2024 ), the no-observed-adverse-effect-level (NOAEL) was determined to be 2 μM for amiodarone and 1 μM for N-desethylamiodarone, while the lowest-observed-adverse-effect-level (LOAEL) was 3 μM for amiodarone and 2 μM for N-desethylamiodarone ( Fig. 2C ). During the metabolism of amiodarone, iodine moieties are released as inorganic iodide, which has been implicated in the drug’s toxicity ( Rao et al. 1986 ; Cardenas et al. 2003 ; Yao et al. 2012 ). To evaluate whether iodide release could underlie the adverse effects of amiodarone and N-desethylamiodarone on mouse gastruloids, we tested the direct impact of iodide exposure. Gastruloids cultured for four days in the presence of increasing concentrations of sodium iodide showed adverse effects only at 20 mM, a concentration substantially higher than the LOAEL determined for amiodarone and N-desethylamiodarone ( Supplementary Figure ). This result suggests that iodide release is unlikely to be the major cause of the observed developmental toxicity of these compounds. 3.2. Morphological impact of dronedarone and its major metabolite on mouse gastruloids Using the same approach as for amiodarone, we evaluated the effects of dronedarone and its major metabolite, N-desbutyl dronedarone, on the morphology of mouse gastruloids. Both compounds caused a dose-dependent reduction in growth and elongation ( Fig. 3A , B ), similar to the effects observed with amiodarone and its metabolite. Morphometric analysis confirmed these trends, with all three parameters progressively decreasing as concentrations increased ( Fig. 3C ). The NOAEL and LOAEL were determined to be 2 μM and 3 μM for dronedarone, respectively, and 1 μM and 1.5 μM for N-desbutyl dronedarone ( Fig. 3C ). 3.3. Effects of amiodarone, dronedarone, and their metabolites on cell viability Previous studies have shown that drug-induced reductions in gastruloid size, quantified by area, often correlate with impaired cell proliferation ( Warkus et al. 2016 ; Warkus and Marikawa 2018 ). To test whether this is also the case for the anti-arrhythmic drugs and their metabolites, we performed a cell viability assay using P19C5 cells cultured in 2D monolayers. The lowest concentrations that significantly reduced cell number were 6 μM for amiodarone, 3 μM for N-desethylamiodarone, 3 μM for dronedarone, and 2 μM for N-desbutyl dronedarone ( Fig. 4 ). These concentrations were comparable to those that adversely decreased the relative area of gastruloids ( Figs. 2 and 3 ), suggesting that reduced cell proliferation contributes to gastruloid size reduction. Notably, however, the elongation of gastruloids, as measured by relative EDI, was adversely affected at even lower concentrations, specifically 3 μM for amiodarone, 2 μM for N-desethylamiodarone, and 1.5 μM for N-desbutyl dronedarone ( Figs. 2 and 3 ). Thus, the adverse morphological effects of these compounds cannot be explained solely by their impact on cell proliferation or viability. 3.4. Impact of amiodarone and dronedarone on gene expression in mouse gastruloids To gain insight into the molecular mechanisms by which amiodarone and dronedarone impair embryonic development, we examined their effects on the gene expression profiles of key developmental regulators in mouse gastruloids. We focused on 4 μM amiodarone and 3 μM dronedarone, as these were the lowest concentrations that adversely affected both growth (area) and elongation (EDI or aspect ratio) ( Figs. 2 , 3 ). We analyzed the temporal expression of 12 developmental regulators, including genes involved in primitive streak formation ( Brachyury , Cdx1 ), paraxial mesoderm formation ( Tbx6 ), somite segmentation ( Meox1 , Foxc2 ), neuromuscular patterning ( Pax3 ), anterior-posterior specification ( Hoxa1 , Hoxc6 , Hoxb7 , Hoxb9 ), and retinoic acid metabolism ( Aldh1a2 , Cyp26a1 ). While amiodarone had minimal effects on some genes, it significantly altered the expression of Meox1 , Foxc2 , Pax3 , Hoxa1 , Hoxb9 , and Aldh1a2 ( Fig. 5A ). The most pronounced effects were seen in Meox1 and Foxc2 , whose peak expression on Day 3 was strongly suppressed by amiodarone. Both genes encode transcription factors essential for proper somite segmentation ( Kume et al. 2001 ; Mankoo et al. 2003 ). Additionally, Aldh1a2 , which encodes the aldehyde dehydrogenase required for retinoic acid biosynthesis (Niederreither et al. 1999; Kumar and Duester 2014 ), exhibited the earliest detectable change, with significant down-regulation observed as early as Day 2. Dronedarone also significantly altered the expression of several genes, showing both similarities and differences from amiodarone ( Fig. 5B ). Like amiodarone, dronedarone markedly reduced the Day 3 peak expression of Meox1 and Foxc2 . It also down-regulated Aldh1a2 on both Days 2 and 3. However, in contrast to amiodarone, dronedarone uniquely suppressed Cdx1 at Day 1 and Tbx6 at Day 2. These findings suggest that while the two drugs share some molecular targets, they also exert distinct transcriptional effects in the mouse gastruloid model. To evaluate the dose-dependent effects of amiodarone and dronedarone on gene expressions, we analyzed Meox1 and Foxc2 expressions in Day 3 gastruloids treated with varying concentrations of each compound. Both genes exhibited progressive down-regulation in response to increasing concentrations of amiodarone and dronedarone ( Fig. 6 ). 3.5. Role of retinoic acid in the morphological impact of amiodarone and dronedarone Both amiodarone and dronedarone down-regulated Aldh1a2 expression in mouse gastruloids on Days 2 and 3 ( Fig. 5 ), potentially reducing retinoic acid biosynthesis. Given that retinoic acid plays an essential role in embryonic growth and axial elongation (Niederreither et al. 1999; Kumar and Duester 2014 ), it is possible that the observed morphological defects caused by amiodarone and dronedarone, namely reduced size and elongation in gastruloids, may be due to diminished retinoic acid signaling. To test this possibility, we supplemented amiodarone- or dronedarone-treated gastruloids with a small dose of retinoic acid (2 nM). This concentration of retinoic acid was used because a prior study ( Huntsman et al. 2024 ) demonstrated that concentrations of 4 nM and above disrupt gastruloid development, in line with the teratogenic nature of excessive retinoic acid ( McCaffery et al. 2003 ). . When mouse gastruloids were treated with both amiodarone (4 μM) and retinoic acid (2 nM) for 4 days, they exhibited robust growth and elongation, with morphological parameters no longer adversely affected, unlike with amiodarone alone ( Fig. 7A , C ). This suggests that reduced retinoic acid biosynthesis is partially responsible for the morphological effects of amiodarone. In contrast, retinoic acid supplementation (2 nM) did not rescue the effects of dronedarone (3 μM). Morphometric parameters remained adversely affected in co-treated gastruloids, similar to those treated with dronedarone alone ( Fig. 7B , D ), suggesting that reduced retinoic acid biosynthesis is not the primary mechanisms underlying dronedarone-induced defects. These findings further highlight mechanistic differences between amiodarone and dronedarone in their effects on mouse gastruloids. 3.6. Impact of amiodarone and dronedarone on a human morphogenesis model We next investigated the potential embryotoxic effects of amiodarone and dronedarone using an in vitro morphogenesis model derived from human H9 embryonic stem cells. While our mouse gastruloid model has been validated according to the ICH S5(R3) guideline using various reference compounds with rodent in vivo toxicokinetic data ( Huntsman et al., 2024 ), human cell-based assays are more challenging to validate due to the scarcity of toxicokinetic data on human embryos. Nonetheless, our human morphogenesis model has demonstrated morphological and molecular responses to known human teratogens, including thalidomide, in alignment with their clinically relevant plasma concentrations ( Marikawa et al. 2020 ). Therefore, assessing the effects of amiodarone and dronedarone in this human morphogenesis model should provide valuable human-relevant insights into their potential impact on embryonic development. Amiodarone exerted a significant morphological impact on the human morphogenesis model at 4 μM, causing cell aggregates to appear smaller and rounder compared to controls ( Fig. 8A ). This observation was supported by morphometric measurements, which showed adverse effects across all three parameters: area, EDI, and aspect ratio ( Fig. 8B ). These morphological alterations appeared to resemble those seen in the mouse gastruloid model under the same concentration of amiodarone ( Fig. 2 ). In addition to morphological changes, amiodarone treatment also altered the expression of key development regulators in a dose-dependent manner. Specifically, the Altered Level of Embryogenesis Regulator Transcript (ALERT) scores, which quantify molecular impact based on the expression profile of 15 selected developmental genes (see Materials and methods ), were 0, 0, 6, and 11 for 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 μM ( Fig. 8C , D ). Accordingly, the NOAEL and LOAEL for amiodarone in the human model were determined to be 1 and 2 μM, respectively. These findings indicate that the human morphogenesis model exhibits a sensitivity to amiodarone that is comparable to the mouse gastruloid model. In the case of dronedarone, cell aggregates treated at the highest concentration (4 μM) appeared smaller and rounder ( Fig. 9A , B ), similar to the effects observed in the mouse model. However, in stark contrast, aggregates treated with lower concentrations (0.25 to 2 μM) displayed highly irregular and convoluted morphologies ( Fig. 9A ). This aberrant shape was unique to dronedarone-treated human cell aggregates and was not observed in the mouse model at equivalent concentrations or in amiodarone-treated aggregates of either species. As a result of this unusual morphological response, the EDI and aspect ratio were markedly elevated, exceeding thresholds for adverse effects ( Fig. 9B ). At the molecular level, low concentrations of dronedarone also caused significant changes in gene expression, reflected by ALERT scores of 9 to 11 across the 0.25 and 2 μM range ( Figs. 9C , D ), aligning with the observed morphological abnormalities. To more precisely assess the potency of dronedarone, additional experiments were conducted using even lower concentrations ( Fig. 9E ). ALERT scores were 0, 1, 2, and 10 for 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 μM, respectively, establishing the NOAEL and LOAEL as 0.025 μM and 0.05 μM. These findings indicate that the human morphogenesis model is substantially more sensitive to dronedarone than the mouse model. 3.7. Genome-wide comparison of transcriptomic changes induced by amiodarone and dronedarone in the human morphogenesis model To further investigate the genome-wide transcriptional effects of amiodarone and dronedarone, we analyzed the transcriptomic profiles of Day 5 human ES cell aggregates treated with 4 μM amiodarone or 0.5 μM dronedarone. These concentrations were chosen because both induced a comparable ALERT score of 11 ( Figs. 8 , 9 ), allowing for direct comparison of their molecular impacts. Microarray analysis comparing control and amiodarone-treated aggregates identified 602 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) with a fold change >1.5, including 323 down-regulated and 279 up-regulated genes ( Table 3 ; Supplementary Data ). Dronedarone treatment yielded 294 DEGs, comprising 207 down-regulated and 87 up-regulated genes ( Table 4 ; Supplementary Data ). Among these, 131 genes were commonly down-regulated and 26 were commonly up-regulated by both drugs ( Fig. 10A ), indicating partial overlap in their transcriptional effects. However, a substantial number of DEGs were drug-specific. Amiodarone uniquely down-regulated 192 genes and up-regulated 253 genes, while dronedarone uniquely down-regulated 76 genes and up-regulated 61 genes. Transcriptional responses of selected DEGs were also validated by quantitative RT-PCR, further supporting the presence of distinct transcriptional effects between the two drug treatments ( Fig. 10B ). These findings suggest that although amiodarone and dronedarone share some molecular impacts, they also elicit distinct gene expression changes, consistent with their differential effects observed at the morphological and functional levels. To further explore the biological processes affected by amiodarone and dronedarone at the transcriptional level, we performed Gene Ontology (GO) term enrichment analysis of the DEGs ( Table 5 ; Supplementary Data ). Genes commonly down-regulated by both drugs were enriched for GO terms related to embryonic patterning and organogenesis, including mesenchyme development (GO:0060485), somite development (GO:0061053), anterior/posterior pattern specification (GO:0009952), morphogenesis of a branching epithelium (GO:0061138), limb morphogenesis (GO:0035108), mesonephros development (GO:0001823), neural crest cell differentiation (GO:0014033), and embryonic digestive tract development (GO:0048566). Genes uniquely down-regulated by amiodarone were enriched for terms associated with muscle and heart development, such as muscle organ morphogenesis (GO:0048644), cardiac muscle tissue morphogenesis (GO:0055008), arterial endothelial cell differentiation (GO:0060842), blood vessel endothelial cell differentiation (GO:0060837), and atrioventricular valve morphogenesis (GO:0003181). In contrast, genes uniquely down-regulated by dronedarone were enriched for GO terms associated with cardiac electrophysiology, namely muscle contraction (GO:0006936) and cardiac muscle cell action potential (GO:0086001). No GO terms were significantly enriched among genes commonly up-regulated by both drugs or among those uniquely up-regulated by dronedarone. However, genes uniquely up-regulated by amiodarone were enriched for distinct GO terms, including cholesterol biosynthetic process (GO:0006695), neurofilament cytoskeleton organization (GO:0060052), and spindle assembly involved in female meiosis I (GO:0007057) ( Supplementary Data ). Although the relevance of these processes to gastruloid development remains unclear, these findings further support that amiodarone and dronedarone elicit distinct transcriptional effects.
discussion: Discussion Anti-arrhythmia medications, amiodarone and dronedarone, have been contraindicated during pregnancy due to concerns about potential embryotoxicity, primarily based on evidence from animal tests. In this study, we evaluated the embryotoxic effects of these drugs using gastruloids — three-dimensional morphogenetic aggregates of pluripotent stem cells — as in vitro models of early embryonic development. Morphology-based endpoint in mouse gastruloids indicated that the lowest-observed-adverse-effect-level (LOAEL) was 3 μM for both amiodarone and dronedarone. In the human morphogenesis model, gene expression-based analysis revealed LOAEL of 2 μM for amiodarone and 0.05 μM for dronedarone. These concentrations are comparable to the plasma concentrations of the drugs observed in clinical settings ( Table 1 ). Thus, the present findings support the utility of gastruloid models in detecting the embryotoxicity of amiodarone and dronedarone. Notably, the human model offers insights into human-specific risks that are otherwise difficult to assess through clinical trials, as pregnant individuals are typically excluded. Our in vitro stem cell-based assays align with a growing regulatory shift away from traditional animal testing for safety evaluation, as exemplified by the recent initiatives by the Food and Drug Administration ( FDA 2025 ) and the National Institutes of Health ( NIH 2025 ), and contribute to the advancement of new approach methodologies (NAMs) for evaluating drug-induced developmental toxicity. In vivo, amiodarone and dronedarone are metabolized primarily in the liver into N-desethylamiodarone and N-desbutyl dronedarone, respectively. Although these metabolites are known to retain pharmacological activities related to anti-arrhythmic effects ( Nattel et al. 1988 ; Heijman et al. 2013 ; Ghovanloo et al. 2016 ; Leow et al. 2023 ), their potential for developmental toxicity has not been previously examined. In vitro assays, including gastruloid models, generally lack metabolic capacity and therefore cannot replicate the biotransformation of parent compounds into their metabolites. However, this limitation also presents an opportunity to test the embryotoxicity of individual metabolites in isolation. In the present study, we found that the LOAELs for N-desethylamiodarone (2 μM) and N-desbutyl dronedarone (1.5 μM) in mouse gastruloids were comparable to, or lower than, those of their respective parent compounds. Importantly, these LOAELs were also close to the plasma concentrations of the metabolites reported in vivo ( Table 1 ), providing the first evidence that these metabolites may contribute to the overall embryotoxic potential of amiodarone and dronedarone. For pharmaceutical compounds, including anti-arrhythmia medications, extensive preclinical and clinical studies often provide detailed information on their metabolic products. However, such data are frequently lacking for non-pharmaceuticals, which are not subject to the same regulatory requirements for metabolic characterization. To address this gap, various in vitro metabolic systems have been explored, incorporating hepatic cells or liver enzyme extracts to simulate metabolic conversions ( Luijten et al. 2008 ; Hettwer et al. 2010 ; Ooka et al. 2020 ; Bouwmeester et al. 2023 ). Nevertheless, the extent to which these systems faithfully reproduce in vivo metabolism, especially in predicting plasma metabolite concentrations relevant to toxicity assessment, remains uncertain and warrants further investigation. While amiodarone showed similar LOAELs in both the mouse (3 μM) and human (2 μM) morphogenesis models, dronedarone appeared markedly more potent in the human model (0.05 μM) compared to the mouse model (3 μM). This observation raises the possibility that the human embryo may be more sensitive to dronedarone than mouse embryos. Because dronedarone has been contraindicated during pregnancy since its initial FDA approval ( FDA 2018 ), there are no published case reports or epidemiological studies in human populations regarding its developmental toxicity. As such, it is premature to draw definitive conclusions about interspecies sensitivity differences to dronedarone. Notably, thalidomide is a well-known example of a drug that exhibits pronounced interspecies differences in embryotoxicity, with extensive in vivo evidence showing teratogenic effects in humans but not in rodents ( Ito et al. 2011 ; Vargesson et al. 2023 ). Consistently, the human morphogenesis model is adversely affected by thalidomide at clinically relevant concentrations ( Marikawa et al. 2020 ), whereas the mouse gastruloid model is not ( Huntsman et al. 2024 ). These findings suggest that gastruloid models may have the capacity to detect interspecies differences in developmental toxicity. Further studies using a broader set of reference compounds tested across both human and mouse models are needed to validate the utility of gastruloids for identifying species-specific toxicological responses. Amiodarone and dronedarone are structurally related anti-arrhythmic drugs, with the primary difference being the presence of iodine in amiodarone ( Fig. 1A ). Dronedarone was developed to reduce the toxicity associated with amiodarone, which was presumed to be caused by its iodine moieties ( Yalta et al. 2009 ; Naccarelli et al. 2011 ). However, the present study showed that dronedarone, despite lacking iodine, still exhibited adverse effects on gastruloids, with potency equal to or greater than that of amiodarone. Our findings also revealed shared as well as distinct effects between these two drugs. In mouse gastruloids, amiodarone and dronedarone induced similar morphological abnormalities, producing smaller and rounder shape, at concentrations between 2–4 μM. They also down-regulated common developmental regulators, such as Meox1 and Foxc2 . However, there were also notable differences. Dronedarone, but not amiodarone, down-regulated Cdx1 and Tbx6 . Additionally, retinoic acid (RA) supplementation alleviated the morphological effects of amiodarone but had no such effect on dronedarone-treated gastruloids. In the human morphogenesis model, dronedarone induced a unique convoluted morphology at low concentrations, an effect not observed with amiodarone. Transcriptomic analysis further revealed both overlapping and divergent gene expression changes, suggesting drug-specific molecular impacts. While inhibition of ion channels and adrenergic receptors, which are shared pharmacological targets of both drugs, may contribute to their embryotoxicity, the observed differences in morphological and transcriptomic outcomes implicate additional, drug-specific toxicological targets. Identifying these molecular pathways will be crucial for guiding the development of safer anti-arrhythmic drugs with reduced developmental toxicity. A growing number of dietary supplements are widely marketed with claims of cardiovascular benefits, including products derived from plants and fungi ( Bronzato and Durante 2018 ; Shah et al. 2019 ; Shaito et al. 2020 ). Given that heart disease remains the leading cause of death, the use of these supplements is likely to increase. However, unlike pharmaceuticals, dietary supplements are not subject to rigorous safety evaluations, including developmental toxicity testing ( Richardson et al. 2022 ). This regulatory gap is particularly concerning for pregnant individuals, who may unknowingly be exposed to compounds with teratogenic potential. With recent regulatory shifts toward reducing animal testing, it is neither feasible nor ethically justifiable to impose traditional animal-based developmental toxicity testing on the rapidly expanding supplement market. To address this challenge, new approach methodologies (NAMs), such as gastruloid-based and other in vitro assays, offer promising alternatives. Despite their potential, however, the routine use of NAMs in regulatory decision-making remains limited. Building confidence in these methods will require continued validation, particularly through studies demonstrating their ability to detect known teratogens such as amiodarone and dronedarone. Such efforts will facilitate the broader integration of NAMs into mainstream toxicological assessments and help ensure the safety of emerging compounds, including dietary supplements, in a way that aligns with modern scientific and ethical standards. The present study employed both mouse and human gastruloids. While human cell-based models are attractive for their clinical relevance, mouse models remain essential for developmental toxicity research. Much of our knowledge of chemical impacts on embryonic development has historically come from animal studies, particularly in rodents, which provide detailed toxicokinetic and toxicodynamic data. The mouse P19C5 gastruloid assay has been validated under the ICH S5(R3) guideline, which was developed primarily on the basis of animal studies in rats and rabbits ( ICH 2020 ), and thus serves as a reliable alternative to rodent-based tests. In contrast, validation of human cell-based models is intrinsically challenging due to the scarcity of in vivo toxicological data from human embryos. Consequently, both human and mouse in vitro models are likely to remain integral components of NAMs aimed at reducing reliance on live animals in developmental toxicity studies. It should be emphasized, however, that NAMs, while highly valuable, are generally insufficient on their own for comprehensive toxicological assessment. The most robust risk evaluation still requires complementary data from animal studies and, where available, human epidemiological evidence. Nevertheless, NAMs offer substantial practical advantages, including lower cost, faster analysis, suitability for mechanistic investigations, and their status as ethical non-animal alternatives, which make them particularly well suited as the first tier of developmental toxicity evaluation, thereby expediting and refining risk assessment.
